network.overall_strategy: This network centers threonine synthesis on the best-supported literature route: surface cyanosulfidic photochemistry generates aldehyde hubs (especially acetaldehyde, lactaldehyde, and glyceraldehyde) from HCN under UV irradiation, after which Strecker and Bucherer-Bergs chemistry produce the threonine aminonitrile and threonine/allothreonine. Hydrothermal chemistry is used conservatively as a source of reduced carbon feedstocks and concentration effects rather than as a direct high-temperature threonine-forming environment, in line with threonine's fragility. Additional literature-supported or explicitly low-confidence auxiliary routes include spark-discharge mixture generation, ammonia-aldehyde hydroxy-amino-acid pools, HCN polymer hydrolysates, and a speculative reverse-aldol relation. Biochemical/prebiotic steps rapidly capture the unstable threonine pool into peptides. The graph is bipartite and connected through shared hubs: HCN, acetaldehyde, lactaldehyde, glyceraldehyde, threonine aminonitrile, and the threonine/allothreonine pool.

molecules.iupac_name: mixed C4 hydroxy aminonitrile/hydroxy amino acid precursor pool

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formate
reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface reduction of CO2 by H2 on Fe-S phases generates formate as a reduced C1 feedstock in vent micropores.
reactions.reasoning: Formate production on Fe-S hydrothermal minerals is consistent with alkaline vent carbon fixation models and FeS/greigite chimney mineralogy; this provides reduced carbon feeding surface chemistry while avoiding direct high-temperature threonine synthesis, which would be disfavored by threonine instability (White et al., 2015; Lane & Martin, 2012; Shock & Canovas, 1998).

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic photoredox generation of formaldehyde, glycolaldehyde, and acetaldehyde from HCN
agents: sulfide minerals (Cu(I) cyanide photoredox mineral system)

conditions.catalyst: Cu(I) cyanide photoredox system with sulfide and phosphate buffer
conditions.other: UV irradiation in shallow surface pool; HCN homologation network

reactions.mechanism: UV-driven Cu(I)/Cu(II) cyanide photoredox cycling with H2S as reductant converts HCN into progressively reduced/homologated carbonyl compounds including formaldehyde, glycolaldehyde, and acetaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: This is the core Sutherland-type cyanosulfidic network linking amino acid and nucleotide precursor synthesis. Ritson and Sutherland showed UV irradiation of Cu(I) cyanide complexes with H2S reductant forms formaldehyde, glycolaldehyde, and acetaldehyde; Patel et al. integrated this into a broader protometabolic network (Ritson & Sutherland, 2013; Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Acetaldehyde cyanohydrin formation
agents: sulfide minerals (Cu(I) cyanide photoredox mineral system)
reactions.mechanism: Nucleophilic cyanide addition to acetaldehyde gives the corresponding cyanohydrin.
reactions.reasoning: Acetaldehyde cyanohydrin is the immediate precursor to lactaldehyde in the experimentally characterized cyanosulfidic threonine route (Ritson & Sutherland, 2013).

reactions.name: Photoreduction of acetaldehyde cyanohydrin to lactaldehyde
agents: sulfide minerals (Cu(I) cyanide photoredox mineral system)

conditions.catalyst: Cu(I) cyanide photoredox chemistry under UV
conditions.other: 2 h UV irradiation; reported 19% yield for lactaldehyde

reactions.mechanism: Photoredox reduction of the cyanohydrin removes the nitrile-derived redox burden and yields 2-hydroxypropanal.
reactions.reasoning: Ritson and Sutherland experimentally demonstrated UV photoreduction of acetaldehyde cyanohydrin to lactaldehyde at 19% yield after 2 h irradiation, making lactaldehyde the best-supported threonine-specific aldehyde precursor (Ritson & Sutherland, 2013). TiO2 is included as a plausible UV-active mineral surface in shallow pools, though the literature-critical reagent system remains Cu cyanide/H2S.

reactions.name: Glycolaldehyde homologation to glyceraldehyde in cyanosulfidic chemistry
agents: sulfide minerals (Cu(I) cyanide photoredox mineral system)

conditions.catalyst: Cu(I) cyanide photoredox system; borate stabilization of sugars

reactions.mechanism: Cyanide-mediated homologation and reduction of lower carbonyls generates a triose pool including glyceraldehyde; borate minerals can stabilize sugar products.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. identified glyceraldehyde as a central branch point in cyanosulfidic protometabolism, and the deep research report specifically notes glyceraldehyde as a direct entry point to threonine aminonitrile. Borate-supported sugar stabilization is consistent with surface prebiotic sugar chemistry (Patel et al., 2015; Islam et al., 2017).

reactions.name: Strecker synthesis of threonine aminonitrile from lactaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: mineral concentration surfaces in aqueous pool
conditions.other: anoxic Strecker conditions; gives diastereomeric/racemic aminonitriles

reactions.mechanism: Lactaldehyde forms an imine with ammonia, followed by cyanide addition to give 2-amino-3-hydroxybutanenitrile.
reactions.reasoning: This is the canonical threonine Strecker step from the literature. Ritson and Sutherland described lactaldehyde conversion to the threonine aminonitrile, and Patel et al. framed it as an inevitable by-product of cyanosulfidic chemistry. The product should be understood as a stereoisomeric pool rather than a single enantiopure compound (Ritson & Sutherland, 2013; Patel et al., 2015; Schroeder & Bada, 1977).

reactions.name: Direct Strecker synthesis of threonine aminonitrile from glyceraldehyde

conditions.catalyst: aqueous Strecker conditions with sugar-stabilizing minerals
conditions.other: surface pool; direct alternate entry point to same aminonitrile hub

reactions.mechanism: Glyceraldehyde undergoes imine formation with ammonia and cyanide addition, giving the same C4 hydroxy aminonitrile connectivity associated with threonine/allothreonine after hydrolysis.
reactions.reasoning: The deep research findings explicitly state that glyceraldehyde + NH3 + HCN provides a second entry point to the threonine aminonitrile. This route creates a key convergence point between sugar and amino acid chemistry (Patel et al., 2015; deep research synthesis citing Sutherland framework).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of threonine aminonitrile to threonine/allothreonine pool

conditions.other: mild aqueous hydrolysis; rapid utilization favored because of instability

reactions.mechanism: Nitrile hydrolysis converts the aminonitrile to the corresponding amino acid pool containing threonine and allothreonine, each racemic.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrolysis of the Strecker aminonitrile is the established final step to threonine/allothreonine. The literature emphasizes that the products are racemic and diastereomeric mixtures and that threonine is prebiotically fragile, favoring rapid downstream capture (Ritson & Sutherland, 2013; Patel et al., 2015; Truong et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Bucherer-Bergs conversion of lactaldehyde to hydroxyethyl hydantoin

conditions.other: Bucherer-Bergs-type setting; thermodynamic sink via hydantoin

reactions.mechanism: Lactaldehyde reacts with cyanide, ammonia, and carbonate/CO2-derived carbamylating species to form a hydantoin intermediate.
reactions.reasoning: Pulletikurti et al. showed Bucherer-Bergs chemistry as a prebiotic route from carbonyl precursors to amino acids via hydantoin sinks; the deep research specifically identifies 5-(1-hydroxyethyl)hydantoin as the threonine-relevant analogue from lactaldehyde, though the threonine-specific case remains inferential relative to pyruvate→alanine (Pulletikurti et al., 2022; Lerner & Cooper, 2005).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of hydroxyethyl hydantoin to threonine/allothreonine pool

conditions.other: mild hydrolysis in concentrated pool/interfacial environment

reactions.mechanism: Ring opening and hydrolysis of the hydantoin yield the corresponding hydroxy amino acid pool.
reactions.reasoning: Hydantoin hydrolysis is the established final step of the Bucherer-Bergs route. For threonine this remains a literature-guided extrapolation from demonstrated analogous systems rather than a fully quantified threonine-specific experiment, but it directly follows the deep research report (Pulletikurti et al., 2022; deep research findings).

reactions.name: Spark-discharge generation of HCN and threonine-relevant aldehyde pool

conditions.temperature: variable atmospheric plasma; aqueous collection at ambient temperature
conditions.catalyst: electrical discharge over reduced gas mixtures; mineral collection surfaces
conditions.other: represents CH4/N2/H2O with traces NH3 or CH4/NH3/CO2/H2S discharge products entering aqueous pool

reactions.mechanism: Electrical discharge over reduced gas mixtures produces complex organic mixtures including HCN and aldehydes such as lactaldehyde-containing pools that subsequently enter aqueous Strecker chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: Ring et al. first confirmed threonine/allothreonine in spark-discharge experiments, and Parker et al. plus Johnson et al. showed H2S-containing and volcanic-style discharge setups enrich hydroxylated amino acids. This node is intentionally framed as a source of precursors rather than a single-step clean threonine synthesis, addressing prior overcompression concerns (Ring et al., 1972; Parker et al., 2011; Johnson et al., 2008).

reactions.name: Conversion of spark-discharge aldehyde pool to lactaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: mineral adsorption/concentration in post-discharge aqueous pools
conditions.other: post-discharge aging and partitioning of aldehyde mixture

reactions.mechanism: Complex discharge-generated aldehydes are concentrated and partitioned into lactaldehyde-containing fractions that can enter Strecker or hydantoin chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: The deep research identifies lactaldehyde as a likely intermediate in discharge-derived threonine formation even when not isolated stepwise. This reaction is a conservative network connector rather than a claim of selective mineral synthesis and is justified to map the experimentally observed discharge mixture onto the resolved threonine pathway (Ring et al., 1972; Parker et al., 2011).

reactions.name: HCN oligomerization to DAMN
reactions.mechanism: HCN oligomerizes through nitrile addition and rearrangement steps to diaminomaleonitrile as a stable oligomer node.
reactions.reasoning: DAMN is the canonical HCN tetramer and a key node in HCN oligomer chemistry. The deep research notes HCN oligomer hydrolysates can yield amino acids including threonine, but only as broad mixtures; therefore this route is explicitly represented as low-selectivity background chemistry rather than a direct threonine step (Ruiz-Bermejo et al., 2013).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of HCN oligomer pool to mixed amino acids including threonine/allothreonine

conditions.other: heterogeneous polymer hydrolysis; low selectivity and mixture-rich

reactions.mechanism: Complex HCN oligomer hydrolysis yields a broad amino acid mixture in which threonine/allothreonine can be minor components.
reactions.reasoning: This is included only as a weak auxiliary route because review literature reports threonine among amino acids found in HCN polymer hydrolysates, but the chemistry is unresolved and nonselective. It should not be interpreted as a target-directed pathway (Ruiz-Bermejo et al., 2013).

reactions.name: Ammonia-aldehyde aqueous chemistry generating C4 hydroxy amino precursor pool

conditions.catalyst: aqueous ammonia with mineral concentration surfaces
conditions.other: mixed aldehyde pool analogous to meteoritic hydroxy amino acid-forming systems

reactions.mechanism: Mixed aldehydes react with ammonia through condensation/rearrangement networks to generate a family of hydroxy amino acid and precursor isomers, including C4 hydroxy amino frameworks related to threonine.
reactions.reasoning: Koga and Naraoka demonstrated that labeled aldehydes plus ammonia generate families of hydroxy amino acids including C3/C4 isomers. This reaction is intentionally represented as an isomer/precursor pool, not as a selective threonine synthesis, correcting prior overstatement (Koga & Naraoka, 2022).

reactions.name: Minor partition of C4 hydroxy amino precursor pool into threonine/allothreonine pool

conditions.other: nonselective mixture resolution; low-confidence auxiliary contribution

reactions.mechanism: Within the broader C4 hydroxy amino acid/isomer pool, some products correspond to threonine/allothreonine structures.
reactions.reasoning: The literature supports production of hydroxy amino acid isomer families rather than clean threonine. This node therefore contributes only a low-confidence, mixture-based path into the threonine/allothreonine pool (Koga & Naraoka, 2022).

reactions.name: Glycine production from HCN-derived chemistry

conditions.other: background amino acid formation in HCN-rich pools

reactions.mechanism: HCN-derived carbonyl chemistry and ammonia produce glycine through standard prebiotic amino acid-forming networks.
reactions.reasoning: Glycine is a ubiquitous product of HCN and discharge chemistry and serves here as the simplest amino acid partner for the speculative retro-aldol-related route and for peptide capture. Its widespread occurrence is supported across classic prebiotic synthesis literature and within the deep research context (Ring et al., 1972; Ruiz-Bermejo et al., 2013).

reactions.name: Speculative reverse-aldol relation: glycine plus acetaldehyde to threonine/allothreonine pool

conditions.other: requires high glycine concentration or catalytic assistance; thermodynamically unfavorable

reactions.mechanism: Conceptual reverse of threonine retro-aldol cleavage, relating glycine and acetaldehyde to threonine/allothreonine skeleton formation.
reactions.reasoning: This route is included explicitly as speculative and low-confidence. The literature identifies the aldol relation but emphasizes that the condensation is thermodynamically unfavorable and lacks a direct purely prebiotic demonstration (Fesko, 2016; deep research findings).

reactions.name: Peptide capture of threonine/allothreonine with glycine

conditions.other: wet-dry cycling on mineral interfaces; rapid capture mitigates threonine instability

reactions.mechanism: Dehydrative peptide bond formation during wet-dry cycles traps the labile threonine/allothreonine pool in short peptides.
reactions.reasoning: Given threonine's poor long-term stability, rapid incorporation into peptides is a chemically sensible fate. Wet-dry cycling and clay-mediated peptide formation are supported by prebiotic polymerization studies, although this exact threonine-glycine dipeptide is a network-level utilization step rather than a threonine synthesis reaction (Lahav et al., 1978; Bujdák et al., 1995; Kitadai et al., 2015; Truong et al., 2019).

pathways.name: Canonical cyanosulfidic lactaldehyde Strecker route
pathways.description: The strongest literature-supported route: HCN photoredox chemistry generates acetaldehyde, which forms a cyanohydrin and is photoreduced to lactaldehyde; lactaldehyde then undergoes Strecker synthesis to threonine aminonitrile and hydrolysis to threonine/allothreonine.

pathways.name: Canonical cyanosulfidic glyceraldehyde Strecker route
pathways.description: An alternate entry from the same HCN photoredox network proceeds through glyceraldehyde, which directly gives the threonine aminonitrile under Strecker conditions before hydrolysis.

pathways.name: Lactaldehyde Bucherer-Bergs route
pathways.description: Lactaldehyde from the cyanosulfidic network enters hydantoin chemistry, forming hydroxyethyl hydantoin, which hydrolyzes to the threonine/allothreonine pool.

pathways.name: Spark-discharge to resolved lactaldehyde-Strecker route
pathways.description: Electric discharge chemistry produces HCN and aldehydes in complex mixtures; the lactaldehyde-containing fraction feeds the resolved Strecker route to threonine aminonitrile and threonine/allothreonine.

pathways.name: Spark-discharge to resolved Bucherer-Bergs route
pathways.description: Discharge-derived aldehyde mixtures feed lactaldehyde into the hydantoin pathway, giving threonine/allothreonine after hydrolysis.

pathways.name: HCN oligomer hydrolysate auxiliary route
pathways.description: A low-selectivity background route in which HCN oligomerizes to DAMN and related polymers whose hydrolysis can yield amino acid mixtures including threonine/allothreonine.

pathways.name: Ammonia-aldehyde hydroxy amino precursor pool route
pathways.description: Mixed aldehydes and ammonia generate a broad pool of C4 hydroxy amino acid precursors and isomers, a small portion of which corresponds to threonine/allothreonine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal-to-surface lactaldehyde Strecker route
pathways.description: Reduced carbon generated in hydrothermal settings is transferred to surface HCN photochemistry, which then follows the resolved lactaldehyde Strecker route to threonine/allothreonine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal-to-surface glyceraldehyde Strecker route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal reduced-carbon feedstocks support surface cyanosulfidic production of glycolaldehyde and glyceraldehyde, which then gives the threonine aminonitrile and threonine/allothreonine.

pathways.name: Speculative reverse-aldol relation route
pathways.description: A low-confidence conceptual path relating glycine and acetaldehyde to the threonine skeleton through the reverse of biological threonine retro-aldol cleavage.
